coredata.dc:title: Influence of edge functionalization on the structural and photophysical properties of graphene quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Zeng, Shitong
Sun, Shihao
Liu, Shan
Liang, Taibo
Lu, Binbin
Guo, Yalong
Zhang, Weichen
Xie, Jianping
coredata.dc:description: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), with unique optical and electronic properties, have gained significant attention for their application in optoelectronics, biomedical imaging, and agriculture. In this study, we examined how incorporating carboxyl, hydroxyl, and epoxy groups into a C54H18 graphene model affected GQD structure, aggregation behavior, and photophysical properties using molecular dynamics simulations and density functional theory calculations. The effects of functionalization on the morphology, energy gap, and light absorption properties of GQDs provided insights into their application as sustainable agricultural agents. The influence of GQDs with different edge functionalization on tobacco plant growth was demonstrated by hydroponic experiments on tobacco seedlings.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Dynamics simulations
Density functional theory calculations
Tobacco plant

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Orbital distribution diagram and molecular transition
Effects of various functional group modifications on molecular morphology
The effect of oxygen-containing groups on monolayer aggregation
Analysis of the radius of gyration
The effect of oxygen-containing groups on the stability of GQDs
Effect of edge functionalization of GQDs on light absorption and energy transfer

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Structure changes of C54H18 graphene after introducing carboxyl, hydroxyl, and epoxy groups.

(a) Rg of C54H18 graphene after the introduction of carboxyl, hydroxyl, and epoxy groups. (b) Rg of C54–18COOH-20OH-3O in an aqueous system and a vacuum system. (c) Intermolecular interaction energy. (d) Intermolecular hydrogen bonding. (e) Interaction energy between GQDs and water. (f) Hydrogen bonding between GQDs and water.
Aggregate state calculation results of the GQDs with different edge functionalization.
HOMO and LUMO energy levels of GQDs with different edge functionalization. Black and red lines indicate HOMO and LUMO levels, respectively.

Characterization of functionalized GQDs. (a) AFM image of GQDs above 10 kDa. (b) AFM image of GQDs between 10 and 100 kDa. (c) AFM image of GQDs below 10 kDa. (d) Fluorescence characterization of GQDs.

The growth-promoting effect of GQDs on tobacco plant growth. Morphology of tobacco plants after 21 days of treatment with GQDs of different molecular weight cutoffs: (a) control group, (b) below 10 kDa group, and (c) above 10 kDa group. Effects of GQDs on (d) tobacco root, (e) tobacco leaf, and (f) whole tobacco plant.
Changes in the three-dimensional size of C54H18 graphene molecules after introducing carboxyl, hydroxyl, and epoxy groups.
Electronic and optical properties of functionalized graphene quantum dots.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Note: Highest Occupied Molecular Orbital (HUMO); Lowest Occupied Molecular Orbital (LUMO); Ground state (S0), Singlet state(S1), Triplet state(T1); The singlet-triplet energy difference (ΔEST).

head.ce:title: Influence of edge functionalization on the structural and photophysical properties of graphene quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Beijing Technology and Business University, Beijing 100048, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aBeijing Technology and Business University, Beijing 100048, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Zhengzhou Tobacco Research Institute of CNTC, Zhengzhou 450001, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Zhengzhou Tobacco Research Institute of CNTC

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bZhengzhou Tobacco Research Institute of CNTC, Zhengzhou 450001, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Beijing Life Science Academy, Beijing 102209, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: cBeijing Life Science Academy, Beijing 102209, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), with unique optical and electronic properties, have gained significant attention for their application in optoelectronics, biomedical imaging, and agriculture. In this study, we examined how incorporating carboxyl, hydroxyl, and epoxy groups into a C54H18 graphene model affected GQD structure, aggregation behavior, and photophysical properties using molecular dynamics simulations and density functional theory calculations. The effects of functionalization on the morphology, energy gap, and light absorption properties of GQDs provided insights into their application as sustainable agricultural agents. The influence of GQDs with different edge functionalization on tobacco plant growth was demonstrated by hydroponic experiments on tobacco seedlings.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene's unique properties have made it a focal point of research across various applications since its initial discovery. Comprising a single sheet of sp2-hybridized carbon atoms arranged in a honeycomb lattice, graphene exhibits remarkable electronic properties, including high charge carrier mobility and semimetal characteristics [1–3]. However, the lack of a bandgap limits graphene's utility in many optoelectronic applications. To overcome this limitation, the development of smaller graphene structures, known as graphene quantum dots (GQDs), has introduced size-dependent effects that enable bandgap formation, thereby expanding potential applications of graphene [4,5]. These nanomaterials are appreciated for their excellent optical properties, low toxicity, and biocompatibility, leading to applications in fields such as optoelectronics, biomedical imaging, and environmental monitoring [5,6]. GQDs have also emerged as a promising tool for enhancing plant growth and photosynthesis, with evidence that they facilitate energy transfer to chloroplasts, thus improving photosynthetic efficiency [5,7]. While advances in genetic engineering and agronomic techniques offer potential solutions for boosting crop yields, these methods can be labor-intensive or raise social concerns regarding the long-term safety of genetically modified foods [8,9]. Despite the promise of GQDs, their preparation with a gradient-tunable quantum yield remains a challenge, although various methods have been developed to enhance the quantum yield of GQDs [10].

In addition to reducing the size of GQDs, functionalization, in which chemical species are introduced into the graphene lattice, has emerged as a powerful tool for modulating the electronic and optical properties of GQDs [11,12]. This method can tailor emission wavelengths and enhance the quantum efficiency, thus paving the way for innovative uses in sensors, biomedicine, and environmental applications. Recent studies demonstrated that edge functionalization generated significant shifts in emission properties, primarily through structural perturbations rather than merely electronic localization [4,13–15]. However, the mechanism by which edge functionalization leads to significant shifts in emission properties is still unclear [16].

In this study, we focused on theoretical calculations to explore how different functional group modifications affected the structure and light conversion efficiency of GQDs. Using a C54H18 graphene model structure, we investigated the impact of carboxyl, hydroxyl, and epoxy groups on the molecular configuration and aggregation behavior of GQDs. By systematically analyzing the structural changes induced by these modifications, including variations in the radius of gyration and three-dimensional measurements, we aimed to elucidate the mechanisms by which GQDs promoted plant growth and enhanced photosynthetic efficiency, paving the way for their innovative application in sustainable agricultural practices.

Graphite plates (purity 99.999 %) were provided by Hebei Qiming Carbon Co., Ltd. (Hebei province, China). Ethylene glycol and polyethylene glycol were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, USA). Ultrapure water was prepared using a Millipore Milli-Q water purification system (Massachusetts, USA). Dialysis bags (500-Da) were obtained from Spectrum Labs (CA, USA). Ultrafiltration tubes of 10 and 100 kDa (Millipore, USA) were purchased from MilliporeSigma (Burlington, MA, USA). A six-cell (27 × 19 × 14 cm) soil-free cultivation box, planting plugs with a diameter of 32 mm, and planting cotton with a diameter of 32 mm were sourced from the Jiyoujia store on Alibaba. Hoagland's nutrient solution was purchased from Beijing Coolab Technology Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). All chemical reagents were at least analytical grade and used directly without further purification unless otherwise stated.

Atomic force microscopy (AFM, SPM-9700, Kyoto, Japan) was used to obtain the surface morphology in tapping mode. Fluorescence spectra measurements were obtained with an RF-6000 fluorescence spectrometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Fluorescence spectra were collected using excitation wavelengths of 420, 445, and 480 nm with a slit width of 5.0 nm for both excitation and emission. An elemental analyzer (Vario ELcube, Germany) was used for elemental quantitative analysis.

A multi-channel programmable power supply, IPMP16–20 L (Chengdu Introtech Technology Co., Ltd., Chengdu, China), was used for controlling the power supply while preparing the GQDs. A 2–16KL low-temperature high-speed centrifuge (Sigma-Aldrich, Germany) was used to separate GQDs of different sizes. An Alpha 1–2 freeze dryer (CHRIST, Germany) was used for freeze-drying GQDs.

Two graphite plates (10 × 10 × 0.5 cm) were inserted into a customized glass electrolytic cell, with one graphite plate as the anode and the other as the cathode. The positive and negative electrodes of the power supply were connected to the graphite plate, and ultra-pure water was used as the electrolyte. The two graphite plates were on the same horizontal plane, the plates were submerged in the electrolyte at a height of 7 cm, the distance between the two plates was 1 cm, the voltage was 15 V, the current was 0.2 A, and the electrolytic time was 5 days.

The obtained electrolyte solution was centrifuged at 12,000 rpm for 30 min, and the supernatant was collected. The supernatant was then subjected to dialysis for 3 days using a 500-Da molecular weight cutoff dialysis membrane. After dialysis, the solution was filtered using a 0.22-µm aqueous phase membrane. Subsequently, ultrafiltration was performed using 10- and 100-kDa molecular weight cutoff ultrafiltration tubes at 7000 rpm for 15 min. This process produced carbon nanocolloids with three distinct molecular weight ranges: <10, 10–100 kDa, and greater than 100 kDa. Finally, the resulting samples were freeze-dried to obtain solid samples.

Atomistic molecular dynamics simulations were performed in the GROMACS [17] (version 2020.6) simulation package, using the general amber force field (GAFF2) and the TIP3P water model. The C54H18 sheet was oxidized along its contour, and then six oxidation states with –OH or –O– groups added randomly on both sides of the sheet were created. The molecules were centered in an approximately 4-nm cubic box, and vacuum molecular dynamics simulations were performed for 10 ns. The OH20-O3 system was also calculated in a water solution with and without protonation of the carboxylate groups. For the self-assembly systems, five sheets were randomly placed in approximately 5-nm cubic boxes and then solvated with water molecules. After 500 steps of energy minimization, the systems were equilibrated under the isobaric (NPT) ensemble, and production runs of 50 ns were performed under the canonic ensemble. The temperature was maintained at 298 K using the Nose–Hoover method, and the pressure was maintained at 1 atm using the Parrinello–Rahman method [18]. The cutoff scheme of 1.2 nm was implemented for the non-bonded interactions, and the particle mesh Ewald method with a Fourier spacing of 0.1 nm was applied for long-range electrostatic interactions. All covalent bonds with hydrogen atoms were constrained using the LINear Constraint Solver algorithm [19].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Orbital distribution diagram and molecular transition
ce:section.ce:para: Density functional theory (DFT) calculations were performed using Gaussian 09 W and GaussView 5.0 software. The calculations used the B3LYP functional with D3 dispersion correction (B3LYP-D3). Initially, the geometry of the structures was optimized using the 6–31g* basis set. Subsequently, single-point energy calculations and Highest Occupied Molecular Orbital (HOMO)–Lowest Unoccupied Molecular Orbital (LUMO) analyses were performed using the 6–311g** basis set. The fluorescence excitation energy and phosphorescence excitation energy were calculated using the TD-DFT method for the S1 and T1 states, respectively.

To initiate the experiment, K326 tobacco seeds were sterilized under UV light for 30 min to remove surface contaminants. They were then immersed in a 10 % NaClO solution for 10 min for further disinfection, then rinsed 5–6 times with sterile water to remove any residual disinfectant. Subsequently, the seeds were treated with 75 % ethanol for 30 s to ensure sterility and air-dried in a laminar flow hood to prevent recontamination.

The sterilized seeds were evenly sown on sterilized MS solid medium plates using sterile toothpicks, with approximately 30–40 seeds per plate. After sowing, the plates were sealed with parafilm and placed in a growth chamber at 28 °C under continuous light for 7–14 days to facilitate germination. This setup ensured controlled environmental conditions that promoted uniform seed germination and early seedling development.

Once the seedlings developed five true leaves after germination, uniform seedlings were selected and transplanted into 4-L hydroponic boxes (six plants per box). Each box contained 2 mL of Hoagland's nutrient solution and 40 mg of GQDs. The experiment included a control group and two treatment groups (<10 and >10 kDa GQD suspensions), with the control group receiving Hoagland's nutrient solution only.

The nutrient solutions were refreshed every 7 days for three weeks. Consistent nutrient and graphene concentrations were maintained across all treatments. Plant growth indicators were measured 21 days after the final treatment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of various functional group modifications on molecular morphology
The effect of oxygen-containing groups on monolayer aggregation
ce:section.ce:para: First, when all 18 hydrogen atoms at the edges of C54H18 were replaced with carboxyl groups (COOH), the edge planar structure was disrupted, resulting in significant deformation, while the internal carbon atoms largely retained their planar structure. This structural change was primarily caused by the relatively large size of the carboxyl groups and their tendency to induce sp2 to sp3 hybridization, which increased bending and irregularities at the edges. However, because these functional groups were concentrated at the edges, the internal carbon atom plane was largely unaffected.

Building on the C54–18COOH structure, the random introduction of 5, 14, and 27 hydroxyl (OH) groups into the internal carbon atom plane progressively disrupted the internal planar structure, producing increasingly greater degrees of wrinkling. This phenomenon was attributed to the introduction of hydroxyl groups, which disrupted the conjugated plane system of carbon atoms, increasing local stress, and initiated a transition from a planar to a non-planar molecular structure. As the number of OH groups increased, the degree of wrinkling intensified significantly. In particular, when 27 OH groups were introduced, the molecule completely lost its planar characteristics and adopted a nearly spherical structure.

Similarly, the introduction of epoxy groups had a pronounced effect on the planar structure. When 20 OH and three epoxy groups were randomly introduced into the internal carbon atom plane of the C54–18COOH structure, the molecule exhibited severe wrinkling and assumed a nearly spherical morphology. This extensive deformation was caused by the combined action of epoxy and hydroxyl groups, both of which induce sp3 hybridization, which disrupted the original planarity and increased the internal stress and distortion. The oxygen content of the molecule reached 52 %, consistent with the elemental analysis of small-sized carbon nanocolloid particles obtained in our experiments, further validating the accuracy of the computational model.

To quantitatively describe these structural changes, the dimensions of the different three-dimensional molecular structures were disaggregated. Table 1 presents the dimensions of each molecule aligned along the three Cartesian axes, where the X and Y dimensions represent the planar size, and the Z dimension corresponds to molecular thickness. The thickness of the C54H18 molecule was 0.339 nm, which was consistent with the typical thickness of a single graphene sheet. After introducing COOH groups at the edges, the planar dimensions increased significantly, and the molecular thickness nearly doubled, indicating a substantial disruption of the planar structure. As 5, 14, and 27 OH groups were randomly introduced into the internal carbon plane of the C54–18COOH molecule, the planar dimensions (X and Y) clearly decreased, while the thickness (Z) increased markedly. This indicated that progressively introducing OH groups disturbed the planar structure, producing significant wrinkling and out-of-plane deformation of the molecule. The greater the number of OH groups, the more pronounced the molecular folding became, causing the thickness to increase substantially. By contrast, introducing epoxy groups produced different structural behavior. While the molecular thickness also increased, the planar dimensions did not show a corresponding reduction. This suggested that the epoxy groups induced a different mode of structural deformation than did the hydroxyl groups, which affected the overall molecular conformation but did not produce the same degree of planar shrinkage.

These results demonstrated that introducing oxygen-containing functional groups disrupted the planar structure of graphene and significantly influenced its aggregation behavior through geometric deformation and electronic structural changes. These computational results provide new insights into the structural transformation mechanisms that occur during the functionalization of graphene quantum dots and their potential for various applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of the radius of gyration
ce:section.ce:para: The radius of gyration (Rg) is a crucial parameter describing the overall size of a molecule, as it reflects the distribution of its constituent atoms around its center of mass. Fig. 2 a illustrates the change in Rg of C54H18 after introducing COOH, OH, and epoxy groups. Upon replacing all 18 edge hydrogen atoms in C54H18 with COOH groups, Rg increased significantly from 0.475 to 0.664 nm. This increase was attributed to the large size of the COOH groups, which disrupted the planar edge structure, causing the molecular framework to expand, while the internal planar carbon structure was maintained.

When hydroxyl groups were randomly introduced onto the internal carbon plane of C54–18COOH in increments of 5, 14, and 27 OH groups, Rg gradually decreased. The introduction of 27 OH groups reduced Rg to 0.612 nm. This trend reflected that the hydroxyl groups disrupted the conjugated planar structure by inducing local stress and structural perturbations, causing wrinkles and folds to form. The greater the number of hydroxyl groups, the more pronounced the folding became, which reduced the overall molecular dimensions. Similarly, adding epoxy groups also decreased Rg, which was explained by the increased folding and disrupted planar structure. The combined effect of hydroxyl and epoxy groups exacerbated the molecular distortion, and the degree of folding increased. These findings indicated that introducing oxygen-containing functional groups induced significant structural changes in the graphene molecules by transforming their planar configurations into more compact, folded geometries. The reduced Rg as hydroxyl and epoxy groups were incorporated highlights the pivotal role of these functional groups in modulating the molecular conformation and potentially influencing the aggregation behavior of the oxidized graphene quantum dots.

In an aqueous environment, water molecules form hydrogen bonds and electrostatic interactions with oxygen-containing functional groups (COOH, OH, and epoxy), which reduces the internal strain within molecules, hinders extensive folding, and leads to a slight expansion in molecular size, as reflected in an increase in Rg. Thus, experiments were conducted, and the results showed (Fig. 2b) that the molecular conformation of C54–18COOH-2OOH-3O in an aqueous system exhibited only subtle changes compared with that in a vacuum system. Rg increased slightly from 0.609 nm in a vacuum to 0.614 nm in solution, indicating that the degree of molecular expansion was not strongly influenced by ionization.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effect of oxygen-containing groups on the stability of GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The stability of GQDs is intricately linked to their molecular edge functionalization. As the number of carboxyl and hydroxyl groups increases, there is a marked rise in intermolecular interaction energy, primarily driven by the formation of hydrogen bonds (Fig. 2c and d). The abundance of these functional groups enhanced the interaction energy and hydrogen bonding between GQD molecules and with the surrounding water molecules (Fig. 2e and f), thereby significantly improving the dispersion stability of the GQDs in an aqueous environment.

However, the presence of epoxy groups had a relatively minor influence on the intermolecular interaction energy and the formation of hydrogen bonds. This suggested that the enhanced stability imparted by edge functionalization was predominantly due to groups such as hydroxyl and carboxyl groups, which actively participate in hydrogen bonding. This insight highlights a critical aspect of edge functionalization in tailoring the physicochemical properties of GQDs for applications requiring high stability, such as biomedical imaging, catalysis, and environmental sensing.

Aggregate state calculations primarily focus on intermolecular interactions, such as van der Waals forces and hydrogen bonds, which play a crucial role in understanding molecular aggregation behavior. Using quantum chemical aggregate state calculations, one can delve into both the properties of molecules in an aggregate state and the factors influencing them. Fig. 3 illustrates the aggregation state of C54H18 graphene after introducing carboxyl, hydroxyl, and epoxy groups. The results showed that C54H18 exhibited an ideal ordered stacking structure; however, incorporating carboxyl, hydroxyl, and epoxy groups at the edges led to a significant collapse of the layered structure. The interactions between the edge groups and the groups on both sides of the plane produced a highly disordered aggregation, which was particularly evident in C54–18COOH-27OH, in which the aggregation of five molecules exhibited a completely chaotic state. This suggested that the highly oxidized graphene quantum dots synthesized in our experiments existed in a form characterized by disordered intermolecular aggregation.

To further elucidate the aggregation behavior, we also obtained data on the size of the aggregates, their intermolecular interaction energies, interaction energies between the molecules and water, the number of hydrogen bonds between the molecules, and the number of hydrogen bonds with water. These data will contribute to a more comprehensive understanding of the behavior of aggregate-state molecules with different functional groups and provide theoretical support for the application of graphene materials.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of edge functionalization of GQDs on light absorption and energy transfer
ce:section.ce:para: The highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO), lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO), energy gap (gap), S0→S1 excitation energy, S0→T1 energy, and singlet-triplet energy splitting (ΔEST) are critical parameters reflecting the efficiency of light absorption, energy transfer, and electron transport in photophysical processes, including photosynthesis. To investigate the effect of graphene quantum dot edge functionalization on light absorption and energy transfer, all relevant parameters were optimized and calculated. As illustrated in Fig. 4 , the functionalized GQDs exhibited a redshift with an increase in carboxyl, hydroxyl, and epoxy groups. The results shown in Table 2 also indicate that with an increasing number of carboxyl and hydroxyl groups on the graphene sheet, the energy gap of the structures (C54, C54-COOH, C54-COOHO-3, C54-COOHO-5, C54-COOHOH-5, C54-COOHOH-14) gradually decreased from 2.81 to 0.52 eV. This suggested that the presence of carboxyl and hydroxyl groups promoted the transition of electrons from the HOMO to the LUMO, rendering excitation more likely. When analyzing the fluorescence and phosphorescence excitation energies of the six structures, it became evident that the increase in carboxyl and hydroxyl groups followed the same trend as that of the energy gap. Consequently, the energy gap and the fluorescence and phosphorescence excitation energies exhibited behaviors that deviated from the trends observed in the previous six structures.

The carbon nanocolloid samples with molecular weights below 10 kDa, between 10 and 100 kDa, and above 100 kDa were prepared by ultrafiltration at 7000 rpm for 15 min using ultrafiltration tubes with 10 and 100 kDa molecular weight cutoffs. The composition of GQDs was analyzed by an element analyzer, and the results are shown in Table 3 . The GQDs were mainly composed of three elements: C, H, and O, indicating that the graphite plate was oxidized in the electrolytic process, with two elements, hydrogen and oxygen, introduced. With the increase in molecular weight, the hydrogen and oxygen content of the GQDs increased, while the carbon content decreased. This indicated that the degree of edge oxidation of the GQDs was inversely proportional to their molecular weight. This trend highlighted the structural influence of oxidation on smaller GQDs, in which a higher degree of edge functionalization was more prevalent. AFM and fluorescence spectra were used to investigate the surface and optical properties, respectively. As shown in Fig. 5 , the GQD samples with varying oxidation levels demonstrated a morphological transition from planar to near-spherical structures as oxidation increased, consistent with theoretical predictions. Furthermore, the fluorescence characterization showed that increased oxidation levels enhanced the fluorescence intensity, aligning with theoretical calculations suggesting that oxidized functional groups promoted conjugation and improved photoconversion efficiency.

In our previous research, we demonstrated that graphene-derived quantum dots have excellent plant growth-promoting effects by enhancing potassium uptake [20]. Some related research showing that graphene-based materials can promote plants grow by increasing the CO2 concentration within the leaf or improving plant resilience and photosynthesis [21,22]. Importantly, comprehensive environmental assessments have revealed that these materials exhibit minimal long-term ecological impact in soil-plant systems and pose negligible risks of human exposure [21]. Thus, to further evaluate the effects of various functional group modifications of GQDs on promoting plant growth, we investigated their influence on the growth of tobacco plants in a hydroponic system. As shown in Fig. 6 , tobacco growth was significantly enhanced by GQDs in the pot experiment. Compared with the control, plants treated with GQDs below 10 kDa demonstrated an increased fresh weight of the root, leaf, and entire plant by 27.10 %, 42.23 %, and 39.92 %, respectively, after 15 days of treatment. Using GQDs above 10 kDa, the fresh weight of the root, leaf, and whole plant increased by 15.57 %, 24.5 %, and 23.41 %, respectively, after 15 days of treatment. Significant differences in the fresh weight of the root and whole plant were observed between the control plants and those treated with GQDs below 10 kDa (Fig. 6). These results indicated that applying GQDs effectively promoted vegetative growth and biomass accumulation in tobacco. In addition, the growth-promoting efficiency of graphene carbon dots was associated with the degree of edge functionalization. This improvement, which may be attributed to the edge functionalization of GQDs enhancing their CO2 absorption and concentration, also boosts rubisco's activities, pigment amounts, light harvesting, and photosynthetic electron transfer, thereby promoting photosynthesis [21]. To better understand the detailed mechanisms, further research is urgently needed.

In this study, theoretical calculations were used to investigate the profound effects of edge functionalization on the structural, electronic, and biological properties of GQDs, thus highlighting their potential in agricultural applications. The results demonstrated that carboxyl, hydroxyl, and epoxy functional groups induced structural transformations, disrupted the planar morphology of the GQDs, and significantly increased molecular wrinkling and folding. In addition, these modifications directly influenced the photophysical properties of the GQDs, including reducing the HOMO-LUMO gap and altering their fluorescence and phosphorescence behavior, thereby improving light absorption and energy conversion efficiency. To validate these changes in optical properties in an application, hydroponic experiments on tobacco seedlings were conducted, which revealed that smaller, highly oxidized GQDs significantly enhanced photosynthetic activity, demonstrating the capacity of GQDs to promote plant growth, consistent with theoretical predictions. This work underscores the versatility of edge-functionalized GQDs, providing a foundation for optimizing their properties for sustainable agricultural practices and broadening their applications in energy and environmental sciences.

We thank the National Natural Science Foundation of China (32,072,344), the Chief Scientist Innovation Projectfrom China National Tobacco Corporation (432022CK0510) for their financial support of our work.
Any additional relevant notes should be placed here.

Shitong Zeng: Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Shihao Sun: Investigation, Conceptualization. Shan Liu: Project administration, Methodology. Taibo Liang: Validation, Supervision. Binbin Lu: Software, Resources. Yalong Guo: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation. Weichen Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Project administration. Jianping Xie: Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors affirm that they are free of any know financial conflicts of interest or personal relationships might have seemed to have influenced the research presented in this study.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We thank the National Natural Science Foundation of China (32072344), the Chief Scientist Innovation Projectfrom China National Tobacco Corporation (432022CK0510) for their financial support of our work.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Tunable optical features of graphene quantum dots from edge functionalization

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Impact of graphene quantum dot edge morphologies on their optical properties

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon dots as light converter for plant photosynthesis: augmenting light coverage and quantum yield effect

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Screening surface-defective graphene quantum dots: promoting plant growth and combating phytovirus
